why_visit: Sacred mountaintop shrine on Mt. Mitake (929m) in Tokyo's Okutama mountains — a genuine mountain pilgrimage just 90 minutes from central Tokyo. A cable car provides access, and a small village of lodgings (shukubo) at the summit makes overnight stays possible. The wolf amulet (inu-hariko) protects homes. Excellent hiking connections to Odake-san and Hinōde-san.
shrine_lore: The wolf deity Ōkuchi-no-Magami ('great mouth deity') guided heroes through the mountains and is celebrated as a protector of the home identical in function to Mitsumine's wolves. Like Mitsumine, the wolf tradition here reflects a pre-Buddhist layer of mountain worship where canines — not foxes — served as divine intermediaries between the mountain world and human settlements below.

domain: Primordial deity; guardian of Sagami Province
deity_lore: The enshrined deity is unusually ambiguous — possibly Amatsumikaboshi, the star deity who refused to submit to heavenly authority and had to be subdued by force; or possibly Kunitokotachi, the primordial earth deity who emerged from the void before Izanagi and Izanami. The mystery of the deity's identity is considered integral to the shrine's spiritual power — a kami too ancient or too profound to be definitively named.
why_visit: Ichinomiya (first shrine) of Sagami Province — the supreme spiritual guardian of all Kanagawa Prefecture. One of Kantō's most visited power spots. The mystery of the deity's identity is itself part of the shrine's appeal — a shrine whose god cannot quite be named draws seekers of the numinous unknown.
shrine_lore: The enshrined deity has been debated by scholars for centuries. The leading theory identifies the deity as Amatsumikaboshi — the star deity who refused to submit to heavenly decrees and had to be subdued by force — or as Kunitokotachi, the primordial earth deity who emerged from the void before even Izanagi and Izanami. The mystery is considered integral to the shrine's spiritual power.

domain: Goddess of Mt. Hakusan; matchmaking, purification & mysterious wisdom
deity_lore: Shirayama-hime (Kukurihime) speaks only once in the Nihon Shoki — mediating between the warring Izanagi and Izanami — and her words are described as 'very mysterious things' that are left entirely unrecorded. She is the goddess of profound hidden wisdom, the deity of words too deep to be written. Her name links to kuku (binding, tying), making her also the goddess of matchmaking — she ties the invisible threads of fate between people.
why_visit: Head of approximately 2,700 Hakusan shrines. Mt. Hakusan is one of Japan's three sacred mountains (alongside Fuji and Tateyama). The shrine grounds feature a beautiful garden and impressive main hall. The ancient pilgrimage route up Mt. Hakusan — begun by the monk Taicho in 717 CE — is one of Japan's oldest mountain climbing traditions, still active today.
shrine_lore: Kukurihime speaks only once in the entire Nihon Shoki, when she mediates between the warring Izanagi and Izanami — and her words are left unrecorded, described only as 'very mysterious things.' She is the goddess of words too profound to be written, of the mediating power that resolves impossible conflicts. Her name is linked to kuku (binding, connecting), making her also a deity of matchmaking and the tying of destinies.

deity: Ōkunitama (+ Emperor Meiji; aspects of Ōkuninushi)
title: Emperor Meiji also enshrined
domain: General divine protection of Hokkaido; prosperity & development
deity_lore: Ōkunitama is an aspect of Ōkuninushi governing the spiritual development and civilization of new lands. At Hokkaido Jingū, this deity was enshrined to represent the divine power of opening and civilizing the northern frontier — Hokkaido's rapid transformation from frontier wilderness to settled province during the Meiji era. Emperor Meiji himself is also enshrined here as co-deity.
why_visit: Most important shrine in Hokkaido, set within Maruyama Park — a forested sanctuary that is Sapporo's finest cherry blossom spot (late April to early May). The approach through dense old-growth trees creates a sacred forest atmosphere. The shrine is integral to Sapporo's identity; the New Year crowds here are enormous and energetic.
shrine_lore: Founded in 1869 when the Meiji government launched the systematic settlement (kaitaku) of Hokkaido, the shrine was established to enshrine the divine power of land development itself — Ōkuninushi's aspect of civilizing the wilderness. Emperor Meiji, who presided over this transformation, was later enshrined here as well. The shrine represents the meeting of Japan's most ancient divine traditions with its most modern nation-building ambitions.

title: Tagorihime, Tagitsuhime, Ichikishimahime
domain: Three sea goddesses; protectors of maritime safety & all roads
deity_lore: The three Munakata goddesses — Tagorihime, Tagitsuhime, and Ichikishimahime — were born when Amaterasu chewed Susanoo's sword and breathed out, three divine daughters emerging from her breath. Amaterasu commanded them: 'Protect the heavenly descendants' mission. You shall be worshipped as the most noble mistresses of all roads.' They governed the sea lanes between Japan and the continent, credited with protecting Yamato court embassies for centuries.
why_visit: UNESCO World Heritage Site (2017). Three shrines: Hetsugū on the mainland, Nakatsugū on Ōshima island, and Okitsugū on the forbidden island of Okinoshima — the last accessible only to male Shinto priests on one day per year (May 27), with mandatory full-body purification on entry. Over 80,000 ritual artifacts from the 4th–9th centuries have been excavated from Okinoshima — so precisely preserved that it's called 'the Shōsōin of the sea.' A place of extraordinary mythological and historical weight.
shrine_lore: The Three Goddesses were born when Amaterasu chewed Susanoo's sword and breathed out — three divine daughters from her own breath. Amaterasu charged them: 'Assist in the governance of the heavenly descendant's mission.' They became the supreme protectors of the sea lanes between Japan and the continent, and their intercession was credited with protecting Yamato court embassies to Tang China and Korea for centuries. Okinoshima's soil itself is sacred — nothing may be removed, not even a blade of grass.

title: — (possibly Kotoshironushi or Hiruko)
domain: God of fishing, commerce, luck & the sea
deity_lore: Ebisu is the only one of the Seven Lucky Gods of purely Japanese origin. In one tradition he is Hiruko — the firstborn boneless child of Izanagi and Izanami, born deformed because their wedding ceremony was incorrectly performed, set adrift in a reed boat and eventually washed ashore as the patron of fishermen. In another tradition he is the son of Ōkuninushi. His jolly, rotund form with fishing rod and sea bream is the most beloved divine image in Japanese commercial culture.
why_visit: Head of all Ebisu shrines. The Tōka Ebisu festival (January 9–11) — marking the end of the New Year season — is one of western Japan's largest events: the 'Fukuotoko' (lucky men) race to be first through the main gate on the morning of January 10, and bamboo branches decorated with lucky charms (fukuzasa) are dispensed to hundreds of thousands of devotees. Extraordinarily lively and joyful.
shrine_lore: Ebisu is the only one of the Seven Lucky Gods of purely Japanese origin. In one tradition he is Hiruko — the first sad child of Izanagi and Izanami, born boneless and deformed because their marriage ceremony was performed incorrectly, set adrift in a reed boat as an infant and washed ashore to become the patron of fishermen. In another tradition he is Kotoshironushi, Ōkuninushi's son, who was fishing when the world changed around him. Whichever origin is true, he became Japan's most beloved deity of everyday luck.

domain: God of fishing, commerce & luck
deity_lore: Ebisu is the only one of the Seven Lucky Gods of purely Japanese origin — either the firstborn boneless child set adrift by Izanagi and Izanami, or a son of Ōkuninushi. He is the god of fishermen, commerce, and luck — eternally jolly, eternally fishing, the divine embodiment of abundance that arrives through patient good fortune rather than grinding effort.
why_visit: One of Japan's three major Ebisu shrines, in the heart of Osaka's historic Namba district. The Tōka Ebisu festival (January 9–11) draws over a million visitors — bamboo branches decorated with fish and treasure charms (fukuzasa) are the defining souvenir. The shrine's surroundings are full of excellent Osaka street food.
shrine_lore: Osaka's centuries-deep merchant culture built itself around Ebisu worship. The plump, eternally smiling god with his fishing rod and sea bream — caught as a lucky windfall, not through grinding labor — perfectly embodied the merchant ideal of abundance arriving through divine favor and commercial savvy in equal measure.

domain: God of fishing, commerce & luck
deity_lore: Ebisu is the only one of the Seven Lucky Gods of purely Japanese origin. He is the god of fishermen, commerce, and everyday luck — eternally jolly and fishing. At Minatogawa he is co-enshrined with the deified spirit of Kusunoki Masashige, the great loyalist warrior of the 14th century, creating an unusual pairing of commercial luck and martial fidelity.
why_visit: Popular Ebisu worship site in Kobe, uniquely co-enshrining the deified spirit of the great 14th-century loyalist general Kusunoki Masashige alongside the god of luck. Near Kobe's Meriken Park waterfront; an easy stop on any Kobe itinerary.
shrine_lore: Kusunoki Masashige — the legendary warrior who fought and died for Emperor Go-Daigo against the Ashikaga forces at the Battle of Minatogawa in 1336 — was enshrined here in the Meiji era as a model of absolute loyalty. The pairing of commercial luck deity Ebisu with the tragic loyalist hero creates an unusual devotional combination that reflects Kobe's identity as both a merchant port city and a place of historical drama.

title: Buddhist: Mahakala; Shinto: aspect of Ōkuninushi
domain: God of wealth, harvests & the kitchen
deity_lore: Daikokuten derives from the Hindu deity Mahakala — a fearsome, skull-garlanded form of Shiva — who was completely transformed in Japan into a smiling, mallet-wielding god of domestic abundance and the harvest. He is often syncretically identified with Ōkuninushi, the great nation-builder. His mallet strikes the ground to release buried treasure; his sacred messengers are rats, who guard grain storehouses from predators.
why_visit: Spiritual guardian of central Tokyo (Edo). The Kanda Matsuri (alternating with Sannō Matsuri, held in odd years in May) is one of Tokyo's three great festivals — mikoshi processions through the business districts of Kanda, Nihonbashi, and Akihabara. The IT industry has adopted Kandamyōjin as its patron deity; tech companies (including major gaming firms) receive special blessings here.
shrine_lore: Daikokuten derives from the Hindu deity Mahakala — a terrifying, skull-garlanded form of Shiva — who in Japan was completely transformed into a rotund, smiling god of domestic abundance. His magic mallet strikes the earth to release buried treasure; his rat messengers guard the rice storehouses (because rats protect grain from predators). He merged with Ōkuninushi, Japan's great nation-builder, making him simultaneously wealthy, kind, and cosmically powerful.

title: Buddhist: Mahakala · Guanyin / Avalokiteśvara
domain: God of wealth & harvests · Bodhisattva of infinite compassion
deity_lore: Kannon (Avalokiteśvara) is the bodhisattva of infinite compassion — the divine being who hears all suffering in the universe and responds with inexhaustible mercy. Kannon can take 33 forms to save all beings. The main Kannon at Hase-dera was carved from the same camphor tree as the one at Kamakura's Hase-dera; the twin was cast into the sea and guided by Kannon's will to Kamakura. Daikokuten is also worshipped here as a secondary deity.
why_visit: Ancient Buddhist temple on a forested hillside in Yamato Province with one of Japan's grandest approaches — a long covered corridor of 399 steps rising through the trees. The Daikokuten hall is one of the finest in Japan. The 10-meter standing Kannon statue carved in the 8th century from a single camphor tree is one of Japan's largest wooden Buddhist sculptures. Spectacular peony garden in spring and autumn foliage.
shrine_lore: This temple shares its founding legend with its twin, Hase-dera in Kamakura: both Kannon statues were carved from the same great camphor tree by the monk Tokudō in 721 CE. One statue was enshrined here in Nara; the twin was thrown into the sea to 'go where the Buddha wills.' It drifted for fifteen years and washed ashore at Kamakura — where the second temple was built to receive it. Two distant temples, one shared origin.

title: Buddhist: Vaiśravaṇa (Kubera)
domain: God of war, treasure & the north; guardian king of dharma
deity_lore: Bishamonten (Vaiśravaṇa) is the Buddhist guardian king of the north — one of the Four Heavenly Kings protecting the dharma. Originally the Hindu deity Kubera, god of wealth. In Japan he stands armored with spear and jewel-pagoda, his fierce expression designed to terrorize all evil. He became particularly associated with military victory and the protection of righteous warriors. At Kurama he is said to preside over Mao-son, a cosmic deity of ultimate power unique to this mountain.
why_visit: Deep mountain temple north of Kyoto accessible by the tiny Eizan Kurama Railway. The forested mountain approach with its massive cedar trees is extraordinary. Kurama is considered a mystical mountain — home to the cosmic deity Mao-son and said to be where the young Minamoto no Yoshitsune trained in swordsmanship taught by tengu (mountain goblins). The Ushiro Festival in October features remarkable ancient rituals by torchlight.
shrine_lore: Bishamonten stands armored with a jewel-spewing pagoda and spear, serving as guardian king of the north against all evil. One of Buddhism's Four Heavenly Kings. In Japan, his terrifying appearance paradoxically made him beloved — the fierceness was reassuring, a powerful defender enlisted on the side of the faithful. Kurama's Bishamonten became especially associated with warrior victory.

title: Buddhist: Vaiśravaṇa
domain: God of war, treasure & the north
deity_lore: Bishamonten (Vaiśravaṇa) is the Buddhist guardian king of the north, standing armored with spear and jewel-pagoda. His wrathful appearance conceals a protective compassion — he fights evil so that humans need not. The monk Myōren's miraculous story of the flying rice barn at Shigisan, depicted in the famous Shigisan Engi Emaki scroll, is the defining legend of this shrine's Bishamonten worship.
why_visit: One of Japan's three great Bishamonten temples, on Mt. Shigi above the Yamato plain. Spectacular views of the plain from the mountaintop. The 12th-century 'Shigisan Engi Emaki' narrative scroll depicting Bishamonten's miracles — including the miraculous flying rice barn — is a National Treasure preserved here and one of Japan's greatest works of narrative painting.
shrine_lore: The monk Myōren prayed to Bishamonten on Mt. Shigi for so long and so intensely that the mountain itself moved — a traveling golden bowl flew to a nearby storehouse and commandeered rice, forcing the wealthy landowner (who refused to donate) to face divine justice. The episode, depicted in the Shigisan Engi scroll with wit and narrative brilliance, became one of the great stories of miraculous faith in Japanese Buddhism.

title: Buddhist: Saraswati (Hindu)
domain: Goddess of music, arts, water, knowledge & beauty
deity_lore: Benzaiten (Saraswati) is the goddess of everything that flows — water, music, time, speech, and knowledge. Originally the Hindu river goddess Saraswati, she was transformed in Japan into a marine deity, a patroness of music and the arts, and the only woman among the Seven Lucky Gods. She is associated with dragons and serpents as masters of water. The five-headed dragon legend at Enoshima is the defining myth of her presence here.
why_visit: One of Japan's three major Benten shrines, on a tidal island connected to the mainland by a causeway. The combination of sea views, cave shrines (Iwaya Caves), dragon legend, and beach culture makes Enoshima uniquely atmospheric. The sea caves below the island, lit with candles, contain carved images with an ancient romance. Excellent view of Mt. Fuji across Sagami Bay on clear days.
shrine_lore: A fearsome five-headed dragon was terrorizing the local villages when Benzaiten descended from heaven on a white cloud. The dragon, captivated by her beauty and moved by her mercy, repented his violent ways and she took him as her husband. The dragon became the shrine's guardian, and the caves where this divine marriage took place are worshipped to this day. Benzaiten is the only woman among the Seven Lucky Gods, and one of the most complex deities in the Japanese pantheon.

title: Buddhist: Saraswati
domain: Goddess of music, arts & water
deity_lore: Benzaiten (Saraswati) is the goddess of everything that flows — water, music, time, speech, and knowledge. The only female deity among the Seven Lucky Gods. At the sacred island of Chikubushima on Lake Biwa, her water-deity nature is expressed in its purest form — an isolated island in Japan's largest lake, worshipped since antiquity as a place where the divine world is especially close to the human.
why_visit: Sacred island in Lake Biwa accessible only by sightseeing boat from Nagahama or Hikone. The entire island — about 2km in circumference — is sacred; no one lives there permanently. One of Japan's three major Benten shrines. The tossing of small clay 'kawarake' discs through a torii gate on the lakeside cliff is a unique ritual offering.
shrine_lore: Benzaiten descended to Chikubu Island from heaven in the distant past, and both Toyotomi Hideyoshi and Tokugawa Ieyasu — usually mortal enemies' legacy holders — both offered special worship and prayers here, drawn by the island's undeniable sacred power. Its island isolation has preserved an atmosphere of absolute otherworldliness, suspended in the middle of Japan's largest lake.

title: Ichikishimahime / Buddhist Benzaiten
domain: Goddess of the sea, arts, beauty & good fortune
deity_lore: Ichikishimahime is one of the three Munakata goddesses, born from Amaterasu's breath. She was identified with Benzaiten (Saraswati) during the medieval period of Buddhist-Shinto syncretism, combining the Japanese sea-goddess tradition with the Hindu river-goddess of music and knowledge. At Itsukushima she governs the sea, arts, and the beauty of the natural world — all three expressed in the iconic floating torii gate.
why_visit: One of Japan's three great views (Nihon Sankei). The floating vermilion torii gate at high tide — with Mt. Misen's forested slopes behind — is among the world's most iconic religious images. UNESCO World Heritage Site. The entire island of Miyajima is sacred; traditionally no births or deaths were allowed on the island. The pagoda, the nō stage, and the shrine corridors built over the tidal shallows are masterpieces of 12th-century architecture.
shrine_lore: Ichikishimahime — one of the three Munakata goddesses born from Amaterasu's breath — was identified with the Buddhist goddess Benzaiten during the medieval syncretic period. Taira no Kiyomori, who rebuilt the shrine in its current magnificent form in 1168, effectively deified this spot as a statement of the Taira clan's supreme power. After the Taira were destroyed by the Minamoto, the shrine's power was so established that it survived its patrons — and became perhaps Japan's most beloved sacred image.

domain: Goddess of wealth, arts & water
deity_lore: Benzaiten (Saraswati) is the goddess of everything that flows — water, music, time, speech, and knowledge. The serpent (ugafuku) is her oldest Japanese form, connecting to the ancient water-serpent deity traditions that preceded her Buddhist identification. At Zeniarai, her power over water is expressed through the sacred spring that 'washes' money into multiplied fortune.
why_visit: Hidden in a cliff cave in Kamakura — entered through a low tunnel bored into the rock face — this tiny shrine in an enclosed grotto is one of Japan's most intimate sacred experiences. The 'money washing' spring (coins washed here are said to multiply when spent) keeps the grotto perpetually busy with businesspeople and believers. A unique cave shrine atmosphere found nowhere else.
shrine_lore: Minamoto no Yoritomo received a dream vision in 1185 on the auspicious 巳 (serpent) day of the serpent month of the serpent year, in which a deity instructed him to build a shrine in the cave where a spring emerged from the cliff. Yoritomo obeyed and his fortunes prospered — establishing the Kamakura Shogunate shortly after. The serpent connection links to Benzaiten's ancient water-dragon associations.

title: Chinese Taoist sage (deified)
domain: God of wisdom, longevity & happiness; enormous forehead containing all wisdom
deity_lore: Fukurokuju is one of three Taoist longevity sages deified in the Chinese tradition and incorporated into Japan's Shichifukujin (Seven Lucky Gods). His enormous elongated forehead contains all the wisdom of the universe; he carries a staff from which hangs a sacred scroll with all wisdom written upon it. He is accompanied by a black tortoise, a crane, and a deer — traditional Chinese symbols of extreme longevity. Some traditions consider him an expression of the Old Man of the South Pole star.
why_visit: Part of the Azabu Shichifukujin pilgrimage circuit, ideal to walk on New Year's week with thousands of other pilgrims. The temple grounds contain two magnificent ancient ginkgo trees — one of which is a designated Tokyo Metropolitan Natural Monument, said to have been planted by the monk Kūkai. A peaceful urban oasis in the embassy district.
shrine_lore: Fukurokuju's enormous domed forehead contains all the wisdom of the universe. He is one of three longevity deities in the Chinese Taoist tradition that were absorbed into Japan's Shichifukujin. He can revive the dead and is accompanied by a black tortoise, a deer, and a crane — all Chinese symbols of extreme longevity. Some scholars consider him an aspect of the Old Man of the South Pole star.

title: Chinese Taoist sage (deified)
domain: God of longevity, good health & wisdom
deity_lore: Jurōjin is another of the three Taoist longevity sages, patron deity of good health and long life. He is distinguished from Fukurokuju by his love of sake — he drinks constantly — and by the deer that follows him. The scroll he carries lists the lifespan of all living things; only Jurōjin knows when each being will die. Some traditions consider him an aspect of Fukurokuju.
why_visit: Enshrines all four founding Shinto pillars (yohashira) and serves as a Shichifukujin pilgrimage site for Matsumoto. Matsumoto itself — home to one of Japan's finest original castle towers — makes this a natural stop. The shrine is in the center of the castle town, ideal for combining with Matsumoto Castle and the city's excellent sake and soba culture.
shrine_lore: Jurōjin is a white-bearded old sage who drinks sake perpetually and loves deer. He carries a sacred scroll that contains the lifespan of all living things — he alone knows when each person will die. Some traditions consider him an aspect of Fukurokuju, distinguished primarily by his more sociable, pleasure-loving nature.

title: Budai — Chinese Buddhist monk (deified)
domain: God of contentment, abundance & good fortune; the Laughing Buddha
deity_lore: Hotei (Budai) was a real Chan Buddhist monk in 10th-century China who wandered with an enormous cloth sack (hotei = cloth bag) distributing sweets and small gifts to children, laughing enormously at everything. He was identified in Japan as Maitreya, the Future Buddha. His perpetual contentment in poverty and his delight in simple kindness make him the most beloved of the Seven Lucky Gods — the god of contentment and the generosity that comes from having nothing to lose.
why_visit: Japan's oldest Zen training monastery (founded 1253, still an active training temple). The famous giant Hotei statue in the Hōjō garden invites visitors to rub his enormous belly for good fortune. The dragon ceiling painting in the main hall (Butsuden) is a masterpiece. The mountain behind the temple provides excellent walking with views over Kamakura.
shrine_lore: Budai was a real historical Chan Buddhist monk in 10th-century China who wandered with an enormous cloth sack (hotei in Japanese) distributing sweets to children, laughing enormously at everything. He was identified in Japan as Maitreya, the Future Buddha. His perpetual contentment in poverty — carrying everything he needed in one sack, delighting in simple pleasures — is considered a teaching in itself.

title: Buddhist: Śrī-mahādevī (Lakshmi)
domain: Goddess of beauty, happiness, fertility & good fortune
deity_lore: Kichijōten derives from the Hindu goddess Lakshmi — goddess of fortune, beauty, and abundance. She arrived in Japan via China as the sister of Bishamonten. Her image at Jōruri-ji, painted on hemp cloth in mineral pigments in the 8th century, shows her in full Tang dynasty splendor: elaborate headdress, jeweled robes, serene expression. She embodies the belief that divine beauty is itself a form of blessing — not vanity, but grace made visible.
why_visit: A hidden gem at the rural border of Kyoto and Nara prefectures. The painted Kichijōten image here — an 8th-century National Treasure of extraordinary delicacy — is displayed only three times a year (New Year period, spring, and autumn). The garden pond temple setting with a nine-story pagoda reflected in still water is perfectly serene.
shrine_lore: Kichijōten derives from the Hindu goddess Lakshmi, goddess of wealth and beauty. She is the sister of Bishamonten. In Japanese Buddhist imagery she is depicted in full Tang dynasty splendor with elaborate headdress, jewels, and flowing robes — one of the finest examples of how Tang Chinese aesthetics were absorbed and perfected in Nara-period Japan. She sometimes replaces Jurōjin in groupings of the Seven Lucky Gods.

title: Buddhist: Lakshmi
domain: Goddess of beauty, happiness & good fortune
deity_lore: Kichijōten derives from the Hindu goddess Lakshmi — goddess of fortune, beauty, and abundance. The sister of Bishamonten. Her painted image at Yakushi-ji (8th century) is one of Japan's oldest and finest Buddhist paintings — a National Treasure depicting the goddess in full Tang dynasty splendor. She represents divine beauty as benediction upon the world.
why_visit: Major Nara temple — one of the great monuments of Japanese Buddhist art. The famous East Pagoda (Tōtō) is described as 'frozen music' for its perfectly harmonious proportions, alternating roofs creating a rhythm unlike any other pagoda in Japan. The Kichijōten painting (shown during New Year period only) is one of Japan's most beautiful ancient Buddhist paintings. The temple has been extensively restored and the grounds are vivid.
shrine_lore: The Kichijōten painting at Yakushi-ji dates to the early 8th century and depicts the goddess in the height of Nara-period luxury — painted in mineral pigments on hemp cloth, her face serene and idealized, her jewelry meticulously detailed. She represents beauty as a form of divine blessing, not mere vanity — the belief that loveliness in the world is itself an expression of cosmic benevolence.

title: Buddhist: Kṣitigarbha
domain: Bodhisattva-protector of children, travelers, pregnant women & the dead
deity_lore: Jizō (Kṣitigarbha) is the bodhisattva who descended into the underworld to rescue abandoned souls, particularly children who died before their parents. On the banks of the Sanzu River (the Japanese equivalent of the Styx), small children who died young are condemned to stack stones for the parents they outlived — but demons knock the stones down endlessly. Jizō hides the children in his robes, whispering sutras, protecting them through eternity.
why_visit: Tokyo Tower rises directly above the temple buildings — one of Japan's most striking visual juxtapositions. The thousands of small Jizō statues in red bibs and knitted hats in the cemetery garden — each representing a child lost before birth — are hauntingly tender. Six Tokugawa shoguns are buried here, and the great Sangedatsumon Gate (1622) is one of Tokyo's oldest surviving wooden structures.
shrine_lore: Jizō (Kṣitigarbha) descended into the underworld specifically to rescue souls abandoned in darkness, especially children who died before their parents. On the banks of the Sanzu River (the Japanese Styx), children who died young are condemned to stack stones — acts of piety for parents who outlived them. Demons knock the stones down. Jizō hides the children in his robes, whispering prayers, protecting them forever from hell's cruelty.

title: Buddhist: Kṣitigarbha
domain: Bodhisattva-protector of children & the dead
deity_lore: Jizō (Kṣitigarbha) is the bodhisattva who descended into hell to rescue the abandoned dead, particularly those who died alone without proper memorial. The monk Kūkai gathered the scattered bones of those left unburied in Sagano and enshrined them collectively — and Jizō stands watch over all the nameless dead, his compassion extending equally to the forgotten and the remembered.
why_visit: Thousands of weathered stone Buddha and Jizō figures rescued from the hillsides of Sagano and enshrined here as a memorial for those who died anonymously in ancient times. One of Japan's most profoundly moving sacred sites. The candlelit ceremony of August 23–24 (Sentō Kuyō) — when all the stone figures are illuminated in the dark — is an experience of rare beauty and weight.
shrine_lore: In ancient times, those who died without family or resources in the Sagano hills were simply left exposed — their bones scattered across the landscape. The monk Kūkai gathered these remains and enshrined them collectively, giving the nameless dead a sacred identity and a place of rest. Over the centuries, additional figures were gathered from across the countryside, creating this vast anonymous memorial — perhaps 8,000 figures — for all the forgotten dead of ancient Japan.

title: Buddhist: Guanyin
domain: Bodhisattva of infinite compassion
deity_lore: Kannon (Avalokiteśvara) is the bodhisattva of infinite compassion who hears all suffering across all realms of existence. The main Kannon image at Kiyomizu-dera is a secret hidden Buddha (hibutsu), displayed only once every 33 years — next in 2033. Kannon can take 33 different forms to save beings in all conditions. The 33-year display cycle mirrors the 33 forms.
why_visit: UNESCO World Heritage Site. The famous wooden stage extending over the cliff — 'jumping from Kiyomizu stage' means to take a bold decisive leap in Japanese idiom — is a masterpiece of timber engineering with no nails. The three streams of Otowa waterfall below grant longevity, love, and study success. Spectacular in cherry blossom season and autumn colors. The sub-shrines — including Jishu Shrine for love, and the 'blind steps' between two stones with eyes closed — are enormously popular.
shrine_lore: Founded in 778 CE by the monk Enchin, who had a vision of a waterfall and was guided to this spot. The main Kannon image is a special hidden Buddha (hibutsu) displayed only once every 33 years — next in 2033. The temple has been rebuilt multiple times after fires; the current structure was completed in 1633 under Tokugawa Iemitsu's patronage. The Kiyomizu Kannon is said to fulfill any sincere prayer made from its famous stage.

title: Buddhist: Guanyin
domain: Bodhisattva of infinite compassion
deity_lore: Kannon (Avalokiteśvara) is the bodhisattva of infinite compassion. The gilded eleven-headed Kannon at Kamakura's Hase-dera — 9.18 meters tall, one of Japan's largest wooden Buddhist statues — represents Kannon in one of its most compassionate forms: the eleven-headed form that can see all directions simultaneously, hearing suffering from every quarter.
why_visit: The gilded eleven-headed Kannon at 9.18m is one of Japan's largest wooden Buddhist statues. The hillside garden in June becomes a breathtaking cascade of hydrangeas in shades of blue, purple, and white. The cave shrine of Benzaiten beneath the main hall is atmospheric. Spectacular views of Kamakura and Yuigahama beach from the hall terrace.
shrine_lore: The statue was carved from a single camphor tree — together with a twin — by the Buddhist monk Tokudō in 721 CE. One was enshrined at Hase in Yamato (Nara). The twin was cast into the sea by Tokudō with the prayer: 'Go where the Buddha wills, to bring salvation to all.' After drifting for fifteen years, it appeared off Kamakura in 736 CE; the temple was built to receive it.

title: Buddhist: Guanyin
domain: Bodhisattva of infinite compassion
deity_lore: Kannon (Avalokiteśvara) is the bodhisattva of infinite compassion and the main deity of Ryōan-ji's inner hall, though the temple is universally known for its karesansui rock garden rather than its principal deity. Kannon's presence here is quietly contemplative — fitting for a Zen temple where the path to compassion runs through sustained attention rather than ecstatic prayer.
why_visit: UNESCO World Heritage Site. The world-famous karesansui (dry rock garden) — 15 rocks arranged in raked white gravel across a 24m × 10m rectangle — is the most famous Zen garden in existence. From no single viewpoint can all 15 rocks be seen simultaneously. Best experienced very early morning before tour groups arrive, seated on the wooden veranda in silence, watching the raked gravel catch the light.
shrine_lore: Founded 1450 CE as a Rinzai Zen temple. The rock garden's authorship and meaning remain unknown — no documentary records survive from its creation. It has been interpreted as islands in the sea, tigers crossing water, islands emerging from clouds, or simply as an unanswerable kōan in stone. Its inscrutability is its teaching: the inability to see all 15 rocks at once from any single vantage is considered a metaphor for the impossibility of perfect comprehension.

title: Buddhist: Acalanātha
domain: God of fire, wisdom & destroyer of evil; immovable guardian
deity_lore: Fudō Myōō (Acalanātha, 'the Immovable One') is a wrathful manifestation of Dainichi Nyorai (the Cosmic Buddha), surrounded by flames, holding a sword to cut delusion and a rope to bind evil. His fearsome expression conceals absolute compassion — he is immovably committed to saving all beings from suffering, by force if necessary. He is the central deity of Japanese Shingon esoteric Buddhism's protective practice.
why_visit: Most visited Fudō Myōō temple in Japan. The goma (Vedic fire ritual) is performed multiple times daily in the main hall — filling the space with chanting, drum beats, and sacred smoke. The historic Narita Omotesandō shopping street preserves a beautiful Edo-era townscape of eel restaurants, craft shops, and pilgrimage inns stretching 800m from the station. The temple park with its three ponds is unusually large and scenic.
shrine_lore: Fudō Myōō (Acalanātha, 'Immovable') stands in raging fire with bulging eyes, holding a sword to cut through delusion and a rope to bind evil. His ferocious expression and blazing aureole represent the wrathful compassion of the Buddha — the commitment to save all beings from suffering by force if necessary. He is the servant of Dainichi Nyorai, the Cosmic Buddha, and his power is invoked specifically to overcome obstacles that cannot be dissolved by gentler means.

title: Buddhist: Acalanātha
domain: God of fire & wisdom
deity_lore: Fudō Myōō (Acalanātha) is a wrathful manifestation of the Cosmic Buddha, surrounded by flames, holding a sword and rope. His fire is the sacred goma flame — burning away all delusion, desire, and obstacle. He is the patron deity of the yamabushi mountain ascetics who practice Shugendō at Mt. Takao, using mountain hardship as a spiritual discipline.
why_visit: Mountain temple on Mt. Takao (599m) — one of the world's most-visited mountains and an easy 50-minute train ride from Shinjuku. Multiple hiking trails of varying difficulty. The fire-walking ceremony (Hiwatari Matsuri, early March) is open to the public — hundreds of ordinary visitors walk barefoot across burning embers after the priests. Autumn foliage from late October through November is spectacular.
shrine_lore: The yamabushi mountain ascetics of Takao-san practice Shugendō — a fusion of esoteric Buddhism, Shinto, and mountain worship that developed in Japan. Fudō's goma fire ritual is the spiritual center of their practice: fire as purification, as the burning away of desire and delusion, as the medium through which divine energy is concentrated and released. Mt. Takao has been a sacred site in this tradition since the 8th century.

title: Buddhist: Acalanātha
domain: God of fire & wisdom
deity_lore: Fudō Myōō (Acalanātha) is the Immovable One — the wrathful compassionate guardian who has stood watch on this mountain since 874 CE. Surrounded by flames of purification, his sword cuts through all spiritual obstacles. The temple's survival through over a millennium of wars and fires is attributed to his unwavering protection.
why_visit: UNESCO World Heritage Site. The five-story pagoda (built 951 CE) is Kyoto's oldest surviving wooden structure — remarkable for having survived wars, earthquakes, and fires over more than a millennium. The Sanbō-in garden, redesigned by Toyotomi Hideyoshi himself in 1598, is one of the finest large gardens in Japan. Hideyoshi held his famous 'Daigo flower-viewing party' here — his last great celebration before death.
shrine_lore: Founded 874 CE by the monk Shōbō on this mountain above Kyoto. Fudō Myōō has been the central deity of worship here for over 1,100 years. The temple's survival through the Ōnin War (which destroyed most of Kyoto), the civil wars, and later conflicts is attributed to Fudō's unwavering protective power — an immovable guardian over an immovable pagoda.

title: Tokugawa Ieyasu (deified)
domain: Deified shogun; divine protector of peace & prosperity
deity_lore: Tōshō Daigongen is the deified spirit of Tokugawa Ieyasu (1543–1616), the shogun who ended a century of civil war and established 265 years of peace in Japan. On his deathbed he asked to be enshrined as the 'divine protector of the east.' He is one of the most recently deified figures in the Japanese tradition — his transformation from mortal general to guardian deity reflects the Shinto belief that great people who transform history retain divine power after death.
why_visit: The most elaborate, ornate shrine complex in Japan — 'never say magnificent until you've seen Nikkō' (nikko wo minaide kekko to iu na). UNESCO World Heritage Site (with Futarasan Shrine and Rinnō-ji). The Yōmeimon Gate with 508 intricate carvings is also called 'Higurashi-no-mon' (Twilight Gate) — so beautiful you could gaze from dawn to dusk and not tire. Famous sights: the three wise monkeys carved above the sacred stable; the Nemuri-neko (sleeping cat); the imaginary elephants carved by an artist who had never seen one. Spring and autumn 1,000-warrior procession festivals reenact Ieyasu's arrival at Nikkō.
shrine_lore: Ieyasu ended over a century of civil war and established 265 years of peace. On his deathbed in 1616, he requested a simple mausoleum at Nikkō — but his grandson Iemitsu (3rd shogun) transformed it into this staggering architectural statement of Tokugawa supremacy, employing 15,000 craftsmen over 2 years. One pillar of the Yōmeimon was deliberately installed upside-down — superstition held that a perfectly completed masterpiece would invite the gods' jealousy and curse. The deliberate flaw preserved the building. Ieyasu's tomb lies at the top of 207 stone steps through ancient cedar forest — austere, dignified, and utterly unlike the theatrical grandeur below.

title: Tokugawa Ieyasu (deified)
domain: Deified shogun; divine protector of peace
deity_lore: Tōshō Daigongen is the deified spirit of Tokugawa Ieyasu (1543–1616), the shogun who unified Japan after a century of civil war. His spirit was enshrined at Kunōzan within 48 hours of his death — an extraordinary urgency reflecting how essential his protective power was considered. He is one of the most recently deified figures in the Japanese tradition.
why_visit: The first Tōshōgū to be built — before Nikkō — constructed within months of Ieyasu's death in 1616. Mountaintop location accessible by ropeway, with spectacular views of Mt. Fuji and Suruga Bay. Far less crowded than Nikkō yet nearly as architecturally splendid. The treasure hall contains Ieyasu's actual weapons, armor, and personal effects.
shrine_lore: Ieyasu died in Sunpu (Shizuoka) in 1616 and was initially interred here at the mountaintop he loved, overlooking the bay he had known since childhood. His spirit was enshrined here as a kami within 48 hours of his death — an extraordinary speed reflecting the political urgency of the moment. The following year, his body was moved to Nikkō per his wishes, but the spirit remains at both shrines simultaneously.

title: Emperor Meiji
domain: Deified emperor of modernization; protector of Tokyo
deity_lore: Emperor Meiji (1852–1912) is the deified spirit of Japan's great modernizing emperor — the man under whose reign Japan transformed from a feudal samurai society into a modern nation-state in a single generation. Unlike most deified humans in Japanese history (warriors and scholars), he is worshipped primarily for peaceful achievement — the vast and bloodless transformation of an entire civilization.
why_visit: Most visited shrine in Japan for New Year — over 3 million people in the first three days. The forested sanctuary of 100,000 trees (planted by volunteers in 1920 from species donated by every prefecture in Japan) is a remarkable urban forest in the heart of central Tokyo. The approach path through ancient cedars creates an immediate sense of transition from the city's noise to sacred calm. The giant wooden torii is one of Japan's largest.
shrine_lore: Emperor Meiji (1852–1912) guided Japan from feudal isolation to modern nation-state in a single lifetime — a transformation of incomprehensible speed and scale. His people enshrined him not through imperial command but through genuine gratitude. The 70-hectare forest surrounding the shrine was a conscious act of nature-creation as sacred offering — designed to last 100 years and be entirely self-sustaining. It has achieved exactly that, and is now an urban old-growth forest of extraordinary ecological value.

title: Emperor Meiji
domain: Deified emperor; outer precincts garden
deity_lore: Emperor Meiji (1852–1912) is worshipped here in the outer precincts (gaien) of his main shrine — the public-facing sacred space dedicated to his memory. The same divine protector of modern Tokyo as at the main Meiji Jingū, but in a more accessible, park-like setting that reflects his legacy of opening Japan to the world.
why_visit: The outer garden of Meiji Jingū — not the shrine itself but its sacred precinct. Famous for the iconic 148-tree ginkgo avenue (300m long) that turns blazing gold in mid-November, one of Tokyo's greatest autumn sights. Contains the Meiji Memorial Picture Gallery and several sports facilities established in Meiji's honor.
shrine_lore: The Gaien was designed as the 'outer' offering space for Emperor Meiji — a place where the public could honor the emperor's spirit through sports, culture, and public gathering. The ginkgo avenue was planned to be visible from the Seisenmon Gate of the inner shrine, creating a visual axis between the sacred forest and the public realm.

title: Toyotomi Hideyoshi (deified)
domain: Deified warlord; god of success from humble origins
deity_lore: Toyokuni Daimyōjin is the deified spirit of Toyotomi Hideyoshi (1537–1598) — the peasant-born soldier who rose from sandal-bearer to ruler of all Japan, the most dramatic social ascent in Japanese history. Posthumously granted the title 'Great Luminous Deity of the Bountiful Land,' he is the patron deity of those who strive to rise above their station through talent and perseverance.
why_visit: Built over Hideyoshi's mausoleum (Toyokuni-byō) on the slopes of Higashiyama. The massive 9-meter wooden sanmon gate — a National Treasure — is one of the grandest shrine entrances in Japan. Conveniently close to Sanjūsangendō, Chishakuin, and the National Museum. The katana, armor, and personal effects in the treasure hall are excellent.
shrine_lore: Toyotomi Hideyoshi rose from a sandal-bearer to a foot-soldier to the unifier of all Japan — the most dramatic rise in all of Japanese history. After his death in 1598, Emperor Go-Yōzei posthumously granted him the title 'Toyokuni Daimyōjin' (Great Luminous Deity of the Bountiful Land). The Tokugawa clan, who seized power after him, suppressed his cult and closed the shrine — it was only re-established in 1868 with the Meiji Restoration. He remains the patron of those who rise above their station.

title: Toyotomi Hideyoshi (deified)
domain: Deified warlord; god of success
deity_lore: Toyokuni Daimyōjin is the deified spirit of Toyotomi Hideyoshi (1537–1598), who rose from peasant origins to unify all Japan. He is particularly associated with Osaka — the great merchant city he rebuilt as the commercial heart of a unified Japan — making him uniquely resonant as the patron of commercial ambition and the dream of social mobility.
why_visit: Directly adjacent to Osaka Castle — Hideyoshi's greatest architectural achievement. A shrine visit combined with the castle grounds and park makes for one of the most historically resonant half-days in Japan. The shrine is modest but charged with the energy of the man who made Osaka Japan's commercial capital.
shrine_lore: Osaka Castle was Hideyoshi's supreme achievement — designed as the impregnable center of a unified Japan, larger and grander than any castle before it. To worship Hideyoshi in the shadow of his own greatest creation connects directly to Osaka's identity: a city of merchants, entrepreneurs, and practical genius that Hideyoshi himself cultivated and embodied.

domain: Gods of nature, purification, rebirth & the cycle of life
deity_lore: The Kumano kami (principally Ketsumiko no Ōkami) are nature deities of extraordinary compassion — unique in Japanese religion for their stated willingness to welcome all people regardless of sin, creed, or impurity. They govern the cycle of death and rebirth, the purifying power of wild nature, and the renewal that comes from immersion in primordial wilderness. Their radical inclusivity made Kumano the most visited pilgrimage in medieval Japan.
why_visit: The spiritual heart of the ancient Kumano Kodō pilgrimage network (UNESCO World Heritage). The enormous torii gate at the original shrine site Ōyunohara — 33.9m tall, 42m wide, the largest torii in Japan — frames a vast gravel riverbed of extraordinary sacred power. The current shrine is deeply atmospheric, forested, and quieter than the gate site. Accessible by bus from Shingū or Tanabe; much better experienced after walking one of the Kumano Kodō routes.
shrine_lore: The Kumano kami extended radical spiritual hospitality: they welcomed ALL who came — regardless of sin, impurity, or creed. Buddhist monks, Shinto priests, criminals, outcasts, and retired emperors alike were accepted. This inclusivity made Kumano the most visited pilgrimage in medieval Japan — retired emperors made the journey over 30 times. The trail was called 'the ant march of emperors' for the constant flow of imperial processions.

domain: Swift sacred deity of purification & speed
deity_lore: The Hayatama deity ('swift jewel') is an aspect of the Kumano gods governing the purifying power of fast-running water — specifically the Kumano River, which races from the sacred mountains to the sea. The name evokes water's ability to cleanse spiritual impurity as swiftly and completely as a river washes a stone.
why_visit: One of the three Grand Kumano Shrines, at the mouth of the Kumano River in Shingū city. The sacred Nagi tree (an ancient podocarpus believed to be 1,000+ years old) stands within the grounds — among Japan's most venerable living sacred trees. The annual Oto Matsuri (water festival in February) features boats on the Kumano River in a ceremony that has continued for over 1,000 years.
shrine_lore: The name 'Hayatama' (swift jewel) refers to the purifying power of fast-moving water — specifically the Kumano River, which the ancient Japanese understood as a sacred channel constantly cleansing the land as it raced from the sacred mountains to the sea. The river itself is a kami, and the shrine at its mouth honors both the water's speed and its cleansing arrival at the ocean.

domain: Spirit of the Nachi waterfall; primal nature deity
deity_lore: The Nachi waterfall deity (Hiryu Gongen) is the divine spirit of the waterfall itself — Japan's tallest at 133m. In ancient Japanese religion, natural features of extraordinary power were understood to be kami directly, not merely symbols of the divine. The thunderous crash of 133 meters of falling water has been heard as the voice of the divine since prehistoric times, making Nachi one of Japan's oldest sacred sites.
why_visit: The view from the pagoda of Seiganto-ji (the adjacent Buddhist temple) with Japan's tallest waterfall (Nachi-no-Taki, 133m) visible through the pagoda frame behind is one of Japan's most iconic and photographed sacred images. The waterfall falls all year without ceasing and the roar of water in the valley is visceral. The 12 Okunchi portable shrine boats paraded through the waterfall mist on July 14 is an extraordinary festival.
shrine_lore: Japan's tallest waterfall IS a kami — not a symbol of one, but the actual divine body of the waterfall deity. The thunderous crash of 133m of falling water has been heard as the voice of the divine since prehistoric times, long before any shrine was built. The Buddhist temple Seiganto-ji beside the Shinto shrine is one of Japan's purest surviving examples of shinbutsu-shūgō — the harmonious fusion of Buddhism and Shinto that characterized Japanese spirituality for over a millennium.

deity: Past / Present / Future life deities (Gassan, Haguro, Yudono)
domain: Three sacred mountains representing past, present & future lives; rebirth
deity_lore: The three mountain deities of Dewa Sanzan represent the past life (Gas-san), present life (Haguro-san), and future life (Yudono-san). Together they govern the Buddhist concept of karmic continuity across lifetimes. Pilgrims who complete all three mountains undergo a ritual of symbolic death and rebirth — making Dewa Sanzan one of the most serious transformative pilgrimages in Japan.
why_visit: Most important mountain worship complex in Tōhoku. Haguro-san's approach via 2,446 stone steps through ancient cedar forest (the oldest cedars over 600 years old, some over 1,000) is awe-inspiring in all seasons and magical in snow. Yudono-san's sacred object is strictly secret — photos and even description are forbidden. The five-story pagoda at Haguro's base is one of Japan's finest wooden towers. The yamabushi monks' 100-day autumn training is among the world's most intensive spiritual disciplines.
shrine_lore: Haguro represents the present life; Gas-san (moon mountain) the past life; Yudono-san the future life — together they form the Buddhist-Shinto pilgrimage cycle of symbolic death and rebirth. Pilgrims who complete all three undergo a transformative ritual death and resurrection. The sokushinbutsu — monks who mummified themselves alive through extreme austerity as the ultimate act of devotion — practiced here; several of their mummies remain in the region, still venerated as living Buddhas.

deity: Various kami of the Ise sacred complex
domain: Full spectrum of kami venerated by the imperial house
deity_lore: The full Ise complex enshrines the complete spectrum of kami revered by Japan's imperial house since antiquity — from Amaterasu and Toyouke in the main shrines to wind gods, moon gods, and agricultural deities in the 125 subsidiary shrines. Together they represent the most comprehensive expression of classical Shinto theology in Japan.
why_visit: Beyond the main Naikū and Gekū, the full Ise complex contains 125 subsidiary shrines including Tsukiyomi-no-miya (moon god), Kazahi-no-miya (wind god), and Takihara-no-miya. A full two-day exploration of the entire complex reveals a comprehensive cosmology of kami in a setting of dense cedar forest and sacred rivers.
shrine_lore: The Ise sacred complex is the most comprehensive expression of ancient Shinto in Japan — the full theological cosmos of kami venerated by the imperial house since the earliest periods of Japanese civilization, arranged across a sacred landscape that has been continuously maintained for over two millennia.

domain: God of the Aso volcanic caldera; sacred volcano deity
deity_lore: Take-iwatatsu-no-mikoto is the descendant of Emperor Jimmu who became the divine ruler of the Aso volcanic caldera — one of the world's largest active calderas. The Aso tradition represents Japan's most ancient volcanic worship, where the god's breath is literally the steam rising from the crater and his anger is the lava flow. This primal identification of deity with active geological force is among the oldest stratum of Japanese sacred belief.
why_visit: One of the oldest and most important shrines in Kyushu, at the foot of the world's largest volcanic caldera. Mt. Naka (active) steams and occasionally erupts in full view behind the shrine — worship amid an active volcano is a primal sacred experience. The main tower gate is being painstakingly restored after severe earthquake damage in 2016.
shrine_lore: The Aso clan deity descended to rule the vast volcanic caldera — one of Japan's most dramatic natural sacred sites. The Aso Jinja tradition of worshipping the volcano reflects the most ancient stratum of Japanese religion, when the raw power of earth and fire was confronted directly without abstraction. The god's breath is the steam; the god's anger is the lava. Faith here is not metaphorical.

deity: Kusunoki-daimyōjin (spirit of the great camphor tree)
domain: God of longevity; spirit of the ancient sacred camphor
deity_lore: The enshrined deity is Kusunoki-daimyōjin — the spirit inhabiting the sacred camphor tree (ō-kusunoki) itself. In ancient Shinto, trees of extraordinary age or size are understood to be shinboku (divine trees) inhabited by kami. This 2,100-year-old camphor tree — 24 meters tall, 23.9 meters in circumference — is the deity, not a home for a deity. It has been worshipped continuously since the reign of Emperor Keikō.
why_visit: A hidden gem near Atami's hot springs — most visitors miss it entirely. The 2,100-year-old camphor tree (Ō-kusunoki) is a National Natural Monument: 24m tall, trunk circumference 23.9m, one of the largest living trees in Japan. Circling the tree once is said to add one year to your life. Combine with a traditional ryokan stay in Atami for a perfect weekend.
shrine_lore: In ancient Japanese Shinto, trees of extraordinary age or size are inhabited by kami — called shinboku (divine trees). This camphor tree has been worshipped since the reign of Emperor Keikō (approximately 1st–2nd century CE). The tree IS the deity — not a symbol, not a home for a deity, but the deity itself made manifest in wood and years. It has witnessed 21 centuries of Japanese history from this riverbank.

domain: God of brewing & mountain water · Sea goddess of safe passage
deity_lore: Ōyamagui-no-Kami is the ancient mountain god of Mt. Matsuo and the supreme deity of brewing — sake, miso, soy sauce, vinegar, and all fermented foods fall under his protection. His sacred spring water (Kame-no-i) is said to prevent sake from spoiling, and sake brewers across Japan travel here to obtain it. The Hata clan, immigrant engineers and brewers from the Korean peninsula who developed western Kyoto, enshrined him in 701 CE. Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto, one of the three Munakata goddesses, is co-enshrined as guardian of seafarers.
why_visit: Kyoto's oldest shrine and the undisputed head of Japan's brewing industry — the stacks of sake barrels donated by breweries from Hokkaido to Kyushu fill the forecourt. The turtle (kame) is the shrine's sacred messenger; turtle motifs appear everywhere. The three Shōfū-en gardens — Mirei Shigemori's final masterpiece, completed 1975 — are among the finest modern gardens in Japan. The treasure hall holds three of Japan's oldest and best-preserved wooden deity statues (Heian period, National Treasures). Camellia trees bloom in spring; the western terminus of Shijō Street marks the entrance.
shrine_lore: The Hata clan — masters of civil engineering who diverted the Katsura River and built the early Kyoto infrastructure — founded the shrine in 701 CE at the foot of Mt. Matsuo where a sacred spring had been revered since antiquity. Their sake-brewing tradition, and the sacred water of Kame-no-i said to render sake unperishable, made Matsunoo the supreme brewing shrine by the Muromachi period. Since then, virtually every sake brewery in Japan maintains a ritual connection here, sending new cedar balls (sugidama) annually. The main honden (1397) in the distinctive matsuo-zukuri style, with porticos fore and aft, is a designated Important Cultural Property.

domain: God of mining, metals, iron & all metalworking industry
deity_lore: God of mining and metals, Kanayamahiko was born from Izanami's vomit (taguri) as she lay dying from Kagutsuchi's birth-fire — her body expelled him along with Kanayamahime as she suffered the burning. He and his sister govern all activities related to metal: mining, smelting, forging, and the craftsmanship of swords and tools. The Gifu hills around Tarui were rich in iron ore, making this location a natural center of metalworking culture since the ancient period.
why_visit: Sōhonsha (総本宮) of approximately 3,000 metal and mining shrines across Japan. The 18 Edo-period buildings rebuilt by Shogun Tokugawa Iemitsu in 1642 (the original complex burned in the 1600 Battle of Sekigahara) are all designated Important Cultural Properties, built in the unique mixed-style 'Nangū-zukuri.' The unusual metal ema (votive tablets) — hoes, sickles, knives, and actual metalwork tools hung as offerings — are found nowhere else. The shrine sits near the ancient Sekigahara battlefield. Five master-smith swords (Sanjō Munechika works) designated Important Cultural Properties are in the treasury.
shrine_lore: The surrounding Ibuki and Nangu mountains yielded high-quality iron, and swordsmiths flourished in this region since antiquity. Every November 8th the shrine holds a swordsmiths' festival — the only Shinto ceremony in Japan dedicated entirely to the art of sword-forging. The entire complex burned on October 21, 1600, during the battle of Sekigahara — the eastern forces of Tokugawa Ieyasu marched through the shrine grounds — and was rebuilt by his grandson Iemitsu as an act of atonement and reverence. The vermilion buildings contrast dramatically with the surrounding cedar forest.

domain: God of nation-building, relationships & the pacification of the land
deity_lore: The same deity as Izumo's Ōkuninushi — Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto — worshipped here for his specific mission of civilizing the Noto Peninsula. According to tradition, Ōkuninushi sailed from Izumo with 300 followers to pacify the monsters (giant birds and serpents) devastating the Noto coast, and after succeeding, remained as the peninsula's guardian deity. As at Izumo, he governs relationships, matchmaking, and the invisible bonds between all living beings.
why_visit: Ichinomiya (first-ranked shrine) of former Noto Province and one of the most important en-musubi (relationship-tying) shrines in the Hokuriku region. The untouched sacred forest (Irazu-no-Mori) behind the main hall has been closed to the public and humans for centuries, creating an atmosphere of primordial sanctity. The Heikoku-sai (Oide Matsuri) in March is one of Japan's largest sacred processions — a divine palanquin travels 300km over 5 nights through Noto. Keta Taisha became especially notable to visitors after the 2024 Noto Peninsula earthquake; visiting supports the region's recovery.
shrine_lore: First mentioned in a Man'yōshū poem by Ōtomo no Yakamochi in 748 CE, and by 859 CE had been accorded the First Court Rank — an unusual honor placing it above even Kashima and Usa in the court's eyes at that time. The Maeda clan, who ruled Kaga Province during the Edo period, were devoted patrons; several current structures date to their governance. The cormorant ritual (U-matsuri, December 16th) — where a living cormorant is carried on foot 40km over three days to become a temporary deity — is one of the strangest and most ancient rituals surviving in Japanese Shinto.
